incidents.headline: Rosneft Tuapse Refinery Black Sea oil spill ongoing contamination reported
incidents.summary: Since April 16, 2026, Ukrainian drone strikes on the Rosneft-operated Tuapse oil refinery in Krasnodar, Russia triggered repeated large fires, spilling hundreds of tons of petroleum products into the Tuapse River and the Black Sea. By May 15, 2026, localized oil spills continued to be found along beaches at Tuapse, Olginka, and Shepsi, with a regional emergency regime still in effect across the Tuapse district. The spill, which at its peak covered at least 10 sq km of sea surface with an oil slick extending 50 km from shore, has caused mass wildlife deaths, toxic 'black rain', benzene/xylene air concentrations three times above safe levels, and a de facto wipeout of the summer tourism season for a 60,000-person city.

secondary_mappings.why: Mass marine wildlife deaths including oiled birds, dolphins, and fish along the Black Sea coast, with ecologists warning of prolonged ecosystem damage.
At least three people killed and dozens injured across multiple strike waves; city-wide school closures and evacuation orders issued.
Environmental disaster is a direct consequence of the Russo-Ukrainian War, with Ukraine targeting Russian oil export infrastructure to deprive the Kremlin of war revenue.
Refinery suspended operations indefinitely, forcing diversion of tanker traffic to alternative Black Sea ports such as Novorossiysk.

incidents.severity_rationale: Regional-scale environmental catastrophe described by ecologists as the worst in the area in years, with hundreds of tons of oil in the Black Sea, toxic air three times above safe limits, mass wildlife mortality, thousands of residents at cancer risk, and indefinite refinery shutdown.

adaptive_objectives.statement: Prevent, detect, and contain unplanned releases of petroleum products or hazardous substances from refinery and marine terminal operations into air, soil, and water, ensuring releases are eliminated or brought within regulatory thresholds within the shortest practicable response window.

adaptive_master_controls.statement: Maintain a documented and exercised Facility Response Plan (FRP) covering worst-case discharge scenarios for all storage tanks, pipelines, and marine loading arms, including pre-positioned containment boom, skimmer assets, and mutual-aid agreements with Tier-2 and Tier-3 response contractors.

Implement continuous ambient air quality monitoring for benzene, xylene, H2S, and combustion particulates at facility fence-line and community receptor locations, with automated alert thresholds linked to public notification and shelter-in-place protocols.

Establish secondary and tertiary containment for all above-ground petroleum storage tanks exceeding 1,000 m3 capacity, including bunded earthworks, sealed drainage, and overflow detention basins rated to hold the full volume of the largest single tank plus firewater runoff.

Maintain pre-contracted marine spill response agreements with qualified Oil Spill Response Organisations (OSROs) capable of deploying within four hours to a worst-case surface spill scenario at the associated marine terminal, including open-sea boom deployment and wildlife rescue capability.

incidents.headline: C&S Chemicals sulfuric acid tank rupture contaminates Poteau River Arkansas
incidents.summary: On approximately May 4, 2026, a tank ruptured at C&S Chemicals in Fort Smith, Arkansas, releasing approximately 86,000 gallons of acidified aluminum sulfate and sulfuric acid into an unnamed creek draining to the Poteau River and ultimately the Arkansas River. The EPA was notified and deployed crews the same day alongside the Arkansas Department of Energy and Environment, the Oklahoma Department of Environmental Quality, and US Fish and Wildlife Services. By May 8, the Arkansas Game and Fish Commission had already confirmed over 300 dead fish near the spill site, a figure expected to increase, with cleanup ongoing as of the sweep window.

secondary_mappings.why: Over 300 dead fish confirmed within days of the spill, with US Fish and Wildlife Services on site to assess ongoing aquatic ecosystem impact.
Multi-agency EPA, state DEQ, and fish and wildlife investigation underway with potential enforcement action against the responsible facility.
Sulfuric acid is a highly corrosive substance; VOC air tests were conducted for workers and nearby community members during active cleanup.

incidents.severity_rationale: Significant localised waterway contamination involving tens of thousands of gallons of corrosive acid, confirmed fish kills across two river systems, and a multi-agency federal and state environmental response, though human casualties were not reported.

sources.title: Fort Smith chemical spill: What we know one week later

adaptive_objectives.statement: Prevent uncontrolled release of hazardous chemicals from storage and processing infrastructure into surrounding environment and waterways.

adaptive_master_controls.statement: Implement and maintain engineered secondary containment systems (berms, liners, retention basins) sized to capture 110% of largest single-vessel volume for all acid and corrosive chemical storage tanks.
Deploy continuous real-time pH and chemical sensor monitoring at all drainage and stormwater outfall points with automated shutoff valves triggered below threshold pH values.
Establish and maintain a Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure (SPCC) plan that is reviewed annually and tested via tabletop exercise with all relevant internal and external response parties.

Mandate periodic structural integrity inspection of all bulk acid storage vessels by a certified third-party engineer, with inspection frequency tied to vessel age, chemical corrosivity, and prior incident history.

adaptive_controls: Install secondary containment berms rated for 110% of the largest acid tank volume at all storage loading dock areas, with drainage routed to a sealed retention basin rather than open storm infrastructure.

Require annual third-party structural integrity inspection of all bulk acid storage tanks using ultrasonic thickness testing, with mandatory replacement triggers documented in the SPCC plan and reported to ADEE.

incidents.headline: Ames Goldsmith Catalyst Refiners toxic gas release kills two in West Virginia
incidents.summary: On April 22, 2026, a volatile chemical reaction at Ames Goldsmith Catalyst Refiners in Nitro/Institute, West Virginia released hydrogen sulfide and nitric acid fumes during tank decommissioning, killing two workers and leaving one in critical condition while hospitalising approximately 30 others including seven first responders. Authorities issued a one-mile-radius shelter-in-place order encompassing West Virginia State University, which was lifted later that afternoon as air monitoring improved. State and federal investigations were opened by the West Virginia Department of Environmental Protection, OSHA, and the EPA; as of the sweep window, results of those investigations had not yet been publicly disclosed.

secondary_mappings.why: Two workers killed and approximately 30 people hospitalised including seven first responders due to direct toxic gas exposure.
Multi-agency investigation opened by West Virginia DEP, OSHA, and EPA; company has prior OSHA citations at its US facilities since 2018.
Governor Morrisey held emergency press conference; company president issued public statement; regional media drew attention to long-standing industrial safety concerns in the Kanawha Valley.

incidents.severity_rationale: Fatal industrial chemical release with two confirmed deaths, one critical, 30+ hospitalisations, and a community-wide shelter-in-place near a university campus; ongoing federal and state investigations elevate regulatory and reputational risk.

sources.title: 2 Dead, Dozens Injured Following Chemical Leak In Institute
Institute chemical leak latest in Kanawha Valley industrial accidents
Two dead after West Virginia chemical spill, dozens hospitalized

adaptive_objectives.statement: Prevent uncontrolled release of hazardous substances into the environment and surrounding community by maintaining process containment integrity and executing safe decommissioning of chemical-bearing equipment.

adaptive_master_controls.statement: Implement and maintain a documented Process Safety Management program covering hazard identification, operating procedures, mechanical integrity, and pre-task hazard reviews for all chemical-bearing equipment decommissioning activities.

Establish continuous atmospheric monitoring protocols including real-time H2S and toxic gas detection with automated alarm thresholds prior to and throughout all confined-space entry and tank decommissioning operations.

Maintain and regularly exercise emergency response and community notification plans, including pre-defined shelter-in-place trigger criteria and coordination protocols with local emergency services and neighbouring institutions.

Conduct pre-decommissioning hazard assessments for all tanks containing or previously containing toxic or reactive chemicals, including chemical compatibility analysis prior to any open, clean, or neutralise operation.

historical_analogues.event_name: DuPont Belle Plant H2S Release
historical_analogues.entity: DuPont de Nemours – Belle, West Virginia
historical_analogues.parallel: H2S release at West Virginia chemical plant killed one worker during routine operations.
historical_analogues.outcome: OSHA cited DuPont for willful violations; $82,500 penalty and PSM programme overhaul mandated.

historical_analogues.event_name: Bhopal Union Carbide Toxic Gas Release
historical_analogues.parallel: Reactive chemical release during non-routine tank operations caused mass community fatalities and shelter-in-place.
historical_analogues.outcome: Catastrophic liability, global regulatory tightening of PSM standards, and eventual plant closure.

adaptive_controls: Require a pre-decommissioning chemical compatibility and reactive-gas risk assessment signed off by a qualified process safety engineer before any tank containing nitric acid residues or sulfur-bearing compounds is opened or purged.

Mandate continuous real-time H2S and NOx atmospheric monitoring with automatic work-stoppage alarms at 10 ppm H2S and evacuation at 50 ppm, with readings logged to a cloud safety platform accessible by the site safety officer remotely.

Enforce a two-person confined-space attendant and standby rescue team protocol during all tank decommissioning activities, with supplied-air breathing apparatus staged and a pre-entry gas test recorded on a signed permit before any worker approaches the tank opening.
incidents.if_you_operate_x_then_y: If you operate a precious metals or specialty chemical refining facility using nitric acid or hydrogen sulfide in any process tank, you should immediately suspend all tank decommissioning activities, verify current confined-space entry permits and atmospheric monitoring protocols, and commission an emergency third-party PSM gap assessment within the next 24-72 hours.

velocity_signal.pattern: Fatal toxic gas release during industrial decommissioning – US chemical/metals sector
velocity_signal.brief: Decommissioning-phase fatalities accelerating in aging US industrial base

incidents.emerging_risk_signal: EPA's February 2026 proposed rollback of SCCAP chemical accident prevention requirements may systematically reduce process safety oversight at US acid-using refiners, amplifying toxic release frequency and community exposure severity.

PHY.source_coverage_notes: Five distinct searches conducted: (1) 'workplace violence attack facility shooting May 2026', (2) 'executive kidnapping assassination attack May 15 16 2026', (3) 'sabotage arson explosion infrastructure site intrusion May 2026', (4) 'shooting stabbing attack security incident May 15 2026', (5) 'fire explosion factory plant warehouse May 15 16 2026'. Sources spanned AP, NPR, PBS, Reuters, Wikipedia, Gun Violence Archive, Just Security, Fire Engineering, and DHS press releases. Seven candidate incidents were identified but all pre-date the May 15-16 window: WHCA Dinner shooting (Apr 25), Mar-a-Lago intrusion (Feb 22), Liuyang fireworks explosion (May 4), Foss High School stabbing (Apr 30), Berlin power grid arson (Jan 3), Tehran Arghavan shopping centre fire (undated), and Vedanta/HPCL India plant incidents (late April/early May). No PHY incident with a confirmed event_date of 2026-05-15 or 2026-05-16 and a specific organisational victim was found in any source reviewed.
